result:
I’ve been thinking lately about the way light behaves when it has no witness—how, long before eyes evolved, photons ricocheted off oceans of cooling magma and no one ever saw the color of that light. It makes me wonder how much of our own inner glare is equally unseen, even by ourselves. We keep a private spectrum that never reflects off another retina, never gets named. Somewhere inside, ultraviolet guilt and infrared longing simply exist, waiting for a receptor that may never evolve.

Tonight the moon is a thin, silver fingernail clipping caught on the dark thumb of the sky, and I keep staring at it as though it’s the bookmark left by someone who closed the universe and walked away. The air smells like wet chalk and lilacs, a combination that shouldn’t work but does, the way certain dissonant chords resolve when the choir holds them long enough. I find myself breathing in deliberate 4/4 time: four counts in, hold, four counts out—an amateur’s antidote to anxiety, a metronome for the blood.

Across the street, the neighbor’s porch light flickers like a hesitant confession. It reminds me of the summer I was nine and convinced the Morse code of fireflies was meant for me alone. I spent whole July evenings translating their yellow flashes into urgent, broken letters: “Stay.” “Run.” “Jump.” I never asked what they were warning me against, only felt the thrill of being elected by something vast and wordless. Now I wonder if childhood is just a long training session in anthropomorphism, and if growing up means firing yourself from the center of every story.

Inside my apartment, the second hand on the wall clock stutters every few ticks, an audible hiccup in time’s otherwise smooth liquor. I keep meaning to replace the battery, but part of me likes the flaw: proof that even the most relentless machinery can limp. I empathize with the pause, the caught breath, the moment where momentum forgets itself. We call this procrastination when we do it; when the cosmos does it, we call it entropy and write equations to excuse the delay.

Someday, scientists will detect the last visible star’s last photon, and the universe will enter its final dark age. I imagine that photon will feel no ceremony—no awareness that it is the ambassador of everything luminous. It will simply travel, untroubled by meaning, until it collides with something that can no longer see. And yet, even in that future emptiness, some version of “here” will persist: the distance between two points, the interval between two non-events, the hush that holds a memory of brightness.

I keep circling back to the idea that the opposite of light is not darkness but isolation. Darkness is just the absence of photons; isolation is the absence of reception. A star in an unpopulated quadrant still shines, but its shine is functionally a secret. Maybe that’s why we conjure gods—cosmic audiences guaranteed never to blink first. Maybe that’s why we conjure lovers, too.

So I leave the porch light on, even though bugs kamikaze against the bulb and the electric bill edges upward. It’s a small, watt-sized prayer: that somewhere a traveler might see the glow and feel a little less lost, that the photons might hit a retina and finally learn what it feels like to be recognized.
